CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early, including the global optimization and syntactic feature additions. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined, with hyperparameters reported and thorough related-work coverage. Figures/tables/examples are effective and informative enough to support claims (datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are clearly presented). Overall writing quality is good, with only minor presentation issues preventing an exceptional (5) rating.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses end-to-end relation extraction, a core NLP task, and targets the machine-learning methods (global training, beam search, LSTMs) that are central to top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) with micro F1 and compares to strong, relevant baselines (Miwa & Bansal, Li & Ji, etc.), uses expected modeling and evaluation practices, and situates itself within the appropriate research community (joint entity+relation extraction). The claimed contributions (global optimization, use of parser hidden layers, LSTM-Minus segments) and reported limitations (beam-size speed tradeoff, no overlapping entities) are appropriate for discussion at a top conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides solid experimental support: evaluations on ACE05 and CONLL04 with comparisons to strong baselines (Miwa & Bansal, Li & Ji, etc.), explicit ablations (syntactic features, LSTM-Minus segment reps) and a clear comparison of global training (beam search with early-update) versus greedy training. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the authors discuss important limitations (beam-size decoding speed and the no-overlapping-entities assumption). Reproducibility is reasonably supported by reported hyperparameters, but lack of mentioned code release and limited exploration of decoding trade-offs beyond beam size 5 leave minor gaps, so the work is strong but not perfectly comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—end-to-end joint entity and relation extraction—and reports state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (ACE05 and CONLL04), which suggests practical significance. Its demonstration that globally normalized training with beam search and early-update improves over locally trained neural models and its incorporation of parser hidden-layer syntactic features provide techniques that other researchers can adopt. The approach is likely to be built upon by the community (structured global optimization for neural IE), though practical limits (beam-size speed tradeoff and the no-overlapping-entities assumption) temper its transformative potential.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Overall the summary indicates the main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on ACE05 and CONLL04 with appropriate baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, etc.) and micro-F1 evaluation. The use of global training with beam search and early-update is a sound technique and the integration of parser hidden layers and LSTM-minus segment representations is plausible and experimentally shown to improve performance. Minor issues remain: no statistical significance testing is reported, code isn't mentioned (hindering reproducibility), and the model assumes no overlapping entities which is an unsupported limitation that could affect generality. These are not fatal to the technical validity, hence a score of 4.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to several strong and relevant baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016; Li & Ji 2014; Miwa & Sasaki 2014; Gupta et al. 2016) on the same datasets (ACE05, CONLL04) using the same micro F1 metrics, and reports ablations isolating parser features, LSTM-minus segments, and global training effects. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the methodology and differences versus baselines are explicitly analyzed (e.g., beam-size tradeoffs and global vs. greedy training). Minor omissions remain: the survey-retrieved literature includes other neural approaches (e.g., instance-attention based neural RE) that are relevant and not explicitly listed among baselines, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper brings together several known components (BiLSTMs, character CNNs, LSTM-Minus segments, and parser hidden representations) and applies global training with beam search/early-update to end-to-end relation extraction, which is a meaningful but incremental synthesis rather than a radical new paradigm. Similar end-to-end neural RE work already exists in the literature (e.g., prior end-to-end neural relation extraction studies and the highly related retrieval result [1]), so the core task formulation is well-studied. The explicit use of parser hidden layers and global beam-trained decoding offers non-trivial engineering and empirical gains, but the techniques largely extend rather than overturn prior methods. Overall, the contribution is moderate novelty: notable integration and empirical advances but not a fundamentally new idea.

main_claims: A globally optimized neural model for end-to-end relation extraction (joint entity detection and relation classification) achieves state-of-the-art results on ACE05 and CONLL04.
Incorporating syntactic features by using hidden layers of a strong parser (Dozat & Manning, 2016) and using segment representations (LSTM-Minus) improves relation extraction performance.

Global training with beam search and early-update improves over locally trained (greedy) neural models, giving better F1; a beam size of 5 is used as a tradeoff between performance and decoding speed.
_summary.methodology: The task is cast as an incremental table-filling problem that jointly labels entities and relations using multiple LSTM encoders (bi-directional word LSTMs and a label LSTM), enhanced by character CNNs, parser hidden-layer syntactic features and LSTM-minus segment representations; the model is trained with globally normalized training using beam search and early-update.
limitations: Decoding speed becomes intolerably slow when beam size increases beyond 5.
Assumes no overlapping entities in a sentence (BILOU scheme with no overlaps).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - end-to-end relation extraction / information extraction
